preamble: The transformation of a palatalized voiceless velar plosive (kʲ) into a voiceless alveolar fricative (s) is a well-attested sound change in historical linguistics, primarily occurring through a process known as sibilantization. This shift is often triggered by the presence of adjacent front vowels or palatal glides, which induce the velar consonant to move forward in the mouth and adopt a fricative manner of articulation.
phoneticEffects: This phonetic shift involves a change in the place of articulation from velar to alveolar (fronting) and a change in the manner of articulation from plosive to fricative (lenition)

examples.note: The Proto-Indo-European palatovelar *ḱ (often considered a palatalized velar) shifted to a sibilant /s/ in Proto-Slavic as part of the Satemization process, exemplified by the word for 'hundred'.

sources: Kortlandt, F. (1994). From Proto-Indo-European to Slavic. Journal of Indo-European Studies.
Britannica, The Editors of Encyclopaedia. (2026, April 11). Palatalization. Encyclopedia Britannica.
